caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The 4-digit 5615 role mix includes OTA/TMC platform roles that are imperfect proxies for the $1–10M EBITDA agency population.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Independent advisors and host-agency contractors may sit outside the supplied W-2 wage base.
inputs.pi_dist: Commission and service-fee economics vary sharply by leisure, corporate, host, and specialty model.
inputs.pi_moat: This is a retained-savings moat judgment, not a terminal-demand score.
inputs.t50_years: The sources cover travelers and broad travel companies, not a representative survey of 561510 lower-middle-market employers.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Not a directly observed code-level adoption rate.
inputs.historical_analogs: BLS occupation-level evidence does not isolate target-band firm economics.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: No code-specific owner-age source was located; the value is not observed and should not be treated as a statistic.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Absence of located evidence is not evidence that succession stress is absent.
inputs.active_consolidators: Evidence is weighted toward larger TMCs and does not establish buyer depth for every agency niche.
inputs.buy_mult: The cited evidence is business-travel/TMC commentary, not disclosed transactions for small host or leisure agencies; normalize owner compensation and gross-versus-net revenue first.
inputs.exit_mult: Requires transferable client relationships and normalized EBITDA; it is not applicable to an owner-dependent or commission-book-only agency.
inputs.pricing_structure: Gross booking and net revenue bases are not comparable across agency disclosures.

q.rationale: The existing v3 distribution-retention × moat-retention judgment is reused as a bounded Stage 1 commercial-retention proxy.

leaves.rationale: Predeclared bounded demand/operator row for the inherited v3 terminal class; unknown classes remain missing.

e.rationale: Global eligible-union share; heterogeneous records use the predeclared wider/lower class-count row.

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $458,147 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561510); class <5: 6,236 firms, avg revenue/firm $469,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,480 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 689 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,063,269 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $322,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 253 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,174,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,331 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 202 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,264,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,293,376 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,659,729 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,424,248 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,919,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $87,470,889 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 250

s5.rationale: Fixed transform of the disclosed v3 owner-age range into five-year qualifying control-transfer propensity.

a.rationale: The selected 0.4994 share reflects AI exposure of information search, booking, servicing, communications, and back office, while preserving advisor judgment, relationship work, physical guiding, and complex exceptions. It is a task estimate mapped to the supplied 4-digit role weights, not an adoption forecast.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $7,729.2M / sectoral output (T30) $36,821.3M, year 2022

rho.rationale: Fixed outcome-blind transform of the v3 t50 adoption estimate, widened by the declared bridge error.

sector.name: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services

source_review.summary: Mechanics pass. The cited evidence supports a high-task-exposure but fragile travel-agency thesis, while the packet candidly treats consumer/company adoption, owner age, small-agency multiples, and the 4-digit labor mix as proxies. Those disclosed limitations warrant publication with caveats but do not establish a score-changing defect.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definitions — Travel Agencies and related travel industries
sources.what_it_supports: Exact 561510 scope as agents selling travel, tour, and accommodation services, plus exclusions for tour operators and other reservation services.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Travel Agents
sources.what_it_supports: Travel-agent task mix, commission/service-fee earnings, personalized-advice demand, and self-booking pressure.

sources.title: The fastest shift in travel behavior just became the default
sources.what_it_supports: Phocuswright-reported 56% traveler use of AI for travel planning, booking, or in-destination assistance in the prior 12 months.

sources.title: The 3 phases of AI adoption—and why some travel companies fall short
sources.what_it_supports: Phocuswright-cited observation that 32% of travel companies were not using agentic AI; this is directional adoption evidence, not a 561510 target-band measure.

sources.title: Business Travel M&A Poised to Double in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Reported TMC seller expectations of 5–7x EBITDA versus a traditional 3–5x range and continued business-travel M&A activity.

sources.title: What the Amex GBT sale means for other TMCs
sources.what_it_supports: Commentary that average TMCs sell for roughly 4–8x profits and that a 10x projected-EBITDA Amex GBT transaction is unusual.

sources.title: Direct Travel Acquires ATPI
sources.what_it_supports: A 2025 managed-travel acquisition and its corporate, leisure, events, and specialized-travel scope.

sources.title: Part-Time Travel Agents Make Up a Third of the Travel Advisor Workforce
sources.what_it_supports: 2023 survey of 251 advisors reporting 64% full-time and 36% part-time participation; it is a labor-structure proxy, not owner-demographic evidence.

title: Travel Agencies
